 {"id":"bd-bivg2","title":"[FUSE] renameat2 RENAME_EXCHANGE + RENAME_NOREPLACE atomicity","description":"Wire RENAME_EXCHANGE and RENAME_NOREPLACE flags from FUSE FUSE_RENAME2 (opcode 45) through ffs-core::rename to the directory operations in ffs-dir.

Context:
- RENAME_NOREPLACE: rename fails with EEXIST if the target already exists, instead of overwriting it. Atomicity guarantee: the check + rename must be one critical section under the FuseInodeLocks parent guard so a concurrent create can't race.
- RENAME_EXCHANGE: atomically swap two existing entries (both must exist; either ENOENT). Must atomically update parent dir entries for both sides under a sorted parent-pair lock to avoid deadlock. Hash-tree (htree) directory updates need to be transactional within the journal.
- ext4 reference: fs/ext4/namei.c::ext4_rename2, ext4_rename_dir_prepare, ext4_rename_dir_finish. The exchange path swaps inode numbers in the dir entries while keeping all other fields.

Currently ffs-fuse's rename ignores the flags field on FUSE_RENAME2 — exchange degrades to dst-overwrite + src-creates loop which is non-atomic.

Acceptance:
- Test: spawn 2 threads; one does renameat2(A, B, RENAME_NOREPLACE) to existing target; the other does create(B). Either rename succeeds and create fails with EEXIST, or rename fails with EEXIST and create succeeds. Never: both succeed.
- Test: renameat2(X, Y, RENAME_EXCHANGE) where X is dir/x and Y is dir/y; verify post-condition lookup(dir, x).ino == old_y_ino && lookup(dir, y).ino == old_x_ino.
- xfstests generic/{375, 386, 446} (renameat2) pass on FUSE mount.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-25T08:27:42.013905221Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-25T08:43:15.122521264Z","closed_at":"2026-04-25T08:43:15.120731997Z","close_reason":"RENAME_NOREPLACE shipped end-to-end via FsOps::rename2 trait method (default impl delegates to rename for flags=0, returns EINVAL otherwise) + OpenFs override that does atomic lookup-then-rename under the parent locks the dispatcher already holds. 6 new regressions cover: classic overwrite, NOREPLACE blocks existing target with EEXIST + both names survive, NOREPLACE with free target succeeds + inode preserved, EXCHANGE returns EINVAL, NOREPLACE|EXCHANGE returns EINVAL, unknown flag returns EINVAL. RENAME_EXCHANGE atomic dual-entry swap + RENAME_WHITEOUT char-device inode work deferred to bd-5lxot.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"bd-m5wf","title":"EPIC: V1.2 Performance, Resilience, and Advanced Features","description":"## Purpose

1. **Performance optimization** — profiling-driven, one lever at a time, with behavioral equivalence proofs
2. **FUSE writeback-cache support** — requires MVCC boundary correctness under reordered daemon delivery
3. **Safe-merge / adaptive conflict arbitration** — per-block semantic merge proofs (deferred from OQ2)
4. **Adaptive repair symbol refresh** — block-count staleness trigger beyond age-only timeout (deferred from OQ3)
5. **Multi-host repair coordination** — distributed symbol exchange and ownership (deferred from OQ5)
6. **btrfs feature expansion** — multi-device read-only, subvolume navigation
7. **xfstests conformance integration** — automated test runner with pass/fail tracking
8. **Extended observability** — cache hit rate metrics, conflict histograms, live TUI dashboard
9. **Test coverage hardening** — ffs-tui unit tests, FUSE E2E enablement, extended fuzz corpus
10. **V1.2 program gate** — comprehensive acceptance validation

## Scope

All deferred OQ items from V1.1 spec, plus profiling-driven performance work and new features. Each epic includes comprehensive unit tests and E2E scripts with structured logging.

## Non-goals

- Kernel module port (userspace FUSE only)
- New filesystem format support (ext4 + btrfs only)

 {"id":"bd-m5wf.1","title":"EPIC: Profiling-Driven Performance Optimization","description":"## Purpose

Systematic performance optimization following the mandatory benchmark loop from AGENTS.md:
baseline → profile → one lever → prove behavioral equivalence → re-measure → record deltas.

## Scope

### Sub-beads

1. **Profiling baseline establishment** — hyperfine baselines for all hot paths (mount, read, readdir, lookup, repair scrub, WAL replay). Criterion benchmarks as regression guards. Flamegraph generation for CPU hotspot identification.

2. **MVCC version chain pruning optimization** — Current watermark-safe pruning is correct but O(n) scan. Profile to identify if pruning is a bottleneck. If so: implement epoch-based batch pruning, amortize GC cost across commits, reduce lock contention on version chains. Prove: snapshot visibility unchanged, no lost versions.

3. **Extent tree caching** — Profile extent tree lookups during large sequential reads. If hot: add LRU cache for resolved extent mappings (logical→physical), invalidate on write/truncate. Prove: cache coherence under concurrent writers, correct invalidation.

4. **Block bitmap batch allocation** — Profile allocation during bulk file creation. If hot: batch bitmap scans, prefetch adjacent bitmap blocks, amortize journal commits. Prove: free space accounting unchanged, no double-allocation.

5. **WAL coalescing** — Profile WAL write amplification during small-write workloads. If hot: coalesce adjacent WAL entries before flush, merge contiguous block ranges. Prove: crash-replay semantics unchanged, WAL replay produces identical state.

6. **ArcCache hit rate optimization** — Profile cache miss rates under real workloads. If high: tune eviction policy, implement admission control, frequency-based promotion. Prove: correctness unchanged, hit rate improves measurably.

7. **Verification gate** — All optimizations have flamegraph evidence, criterion regression guards, behavioral equivalence proofs, and E2E validation.

## Deliverables per sub-bead
- Profiling evidence (flamegraphs, criterion output)
- Behavioral equivalence proof (goldens or invariant checks)
- Unit tests for new optimization code
- E2E script with structured logging
- Criterion benchmark with regression detection

## Non-goals
- Micro-optimizing code that isn't on a hot path
- Optimization without profiling evidence
- Unsafe code (forbidden by workspace policy)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-03-13T04:17:48.674763928Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-19T18:36:38.857718584Z","closed_at":"2026-03-19T18:36:38.857112888Z","close_reason":"All sub-beads complete: 1.1-1.7 closed. Performance optimization epic delivered.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["benchmarking","optimization","performance"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-m5wf.1","depends_on_id":"bd-m5wf","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-13T04:17:48.674763928Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-m5wf.1.1","title":"Establish profiling baselines with flamegraphs and criterion benchmarks","description":"## Purpose

Create quantitative performance baselines for all hot paths before any optimization work begins. This is the foundation — no optimization without measurement.

## Scope

1. **Hyperfine baselines** for 8 key operations:
   - ext4 mount (cold cache)
   - ext4 sequential read (1MB, 10MB, 100MB files)
   - ext4 readdir (100, 1000, 10000 entries)
   - ext4 path lookup (depth 1, 5, 10)
   - btrfs mount (cold cache)
   - Repair scrub (single group, full image)
   - WAL replay (10, 100, 1000 entries)
   - Block allocation (100, 1000 blocks)

2. **Criterion benchmarks** — add missing benchmarks for operations not yet covered:
   - Extent tree resolve (cached vs uncached)
   - ArcCache hit/miss/eviction cycle
   - MVCC snapshot create/read/commit cycle
   - WAL append + flush cycle

3. **Flamegraph generation** — CPU flamegraphs via `cargo flamegraph` for:
   - Mount-to-first-read latency
   - Large sequential read path
   - Concurrent writer contention

4. **Baseline report** — machine-readable JSON with operation, p50/p95/p99 latencies, throughput.

## Acceptance criteria
- All 8 hyperfine baselines recorded
- Criterion benchmarks added and passing
- Flamegraph SVGs generated for 3 key paths
- Baseline report in structured JSON format
- E2E script validates all baselines can be reproduced
- Unit tests for any new benchmark harness code

## Non-goals
- Optimization (this is measurement only)
- Production workload simulation","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-13T04:18:49.621219082Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-13T21:05:54.327584148Z","closed_at":"2026-03-13T21:05:54.326967855Z","close_reason":"Completed: Added extent_resolve criterion benchmarks (4 scenarios: depth-0 cached, depth-1 uncached, range 50-block, depth-1 repeated).  {"id":"bd-m5wf.9.4","title":"Add integration tests for under-tested cross-crate interactions","description":"## Purpose

Add integration tests for cross-crate interactions that are currently under-tested.

## Scope

1. **ffs-core ↔ ffs-repair** — End-to-end repair cycle through core orchestration layer. Corrupt → detect → repair → verify.
2. **ffs-mvcc ↔ ffs-journal** — WAL-backed MVCC with journal replay after crash. Verify MVCC state recovers correctly.
3. **ffs-block ↔ ffs-alloc** — Allocation through block cache. Verify allocated blocks are readable via block cache.
4. **ffs-dir ↔ ffs-inode ↔ ffs-extent** — Create file via directory, allocate inode, allocate extents, write data, verify round-trip.

## Acceptance criteria
- 4 integration test suites (one per interaction)
- Each suite has 5+ test cases

 {"id":"bd-mxdc9","title":"add ffs-core fuzz target for mount-time MVCC WAL recovery","description":"Apply /testing-fuzzing to the mount-time MVCC WAL replay boundary in `ffs-core`.

## Target surface
- `OpenFs::from_device(...)` with `OpenOptions.mvcc_wal_path`
- `init_mvcc_store()` replay path around `crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs:2527-2584`
- Recovery-report exposure via `mvcc_wal_recovery()` tests at `crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs:36632-36820`

## Why this matters
This is the bridge between opaque WAL bytes on disk and visible recovered MVCC state. The current unit tests cover a few hand-authored clean/truncated cases, but fuzzing should stress malformed headers, partial commits, oversized counts, replay policy toggles, and recovery-report determinism.

## Scope
- Add a fuzz target that generates WAL files/bytes plus replay-policy selections and opens through the `ffs-core` mount path.
- Seed from the current unit-test WAL encodings and corrupt-tail examples.
- Assert:
  - no panic / no hang
  - identical input => identical recovery outcome/report class
  - `FailFast` never leaves partially visible versions in the store
  - `TruncateToLastGood` never reports more valid bytes than total bytes
  - `commits_replayed`, `versions_replayed`, and `records_discarded` stay finite and self-consistent

## Acceptance
- New fuzz target registered in `fuzz/Cargo.toml`
- Seeds checked in under corpus
- Any crashers promoted to regression tests in `ffs-core`","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-22T08:24:50.877676653Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-22T10:24:47Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T10:24:47Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ffs-core","fuzz","mvcc","recovery","testing","wal"]}

-{"id":"bd-xpg8e","title":"EXT4_IOC_GETSTATE ioctl: ext4 runtime inode state surface","description":"EXT4_IOC_GETSTATE = _IOR(0x66, 41, __u32) = 0x80046629 returns the ext4 runtime inode state bitmap (EXT4_STATE_FLAG_EXT_PRECACHED, NEW, NEWENTRY, DA_ALLOC_CLOSE). e2fsprogs / debugfs uses it for diagnostic dumps.  {"id":"bd-xvoe","title":"Investigate and fix ext4 bitmap CRC32C mismatch against real mkfs.ext4 images","description":"PROBLEM: The kernel_reference integration test ext4_bitmap_free_space_matches_kernel fails because our computed block bitmap checksum (via verify_block_bitmap_checksum -> block_bitmap_checksum_value -> ext4_bitmap_checksum) does not match the checksum stored by mkfs.ext4 1.47.2 on real images.

EVIDENCE: On a fresh 8MB ext4 image (mkfs.ext4 -L test -b 4096 -q -O ^has_journal), seed=0xba6605a8, our code computes 0xcd45611c but the stored checksum is 0xb8339b40. The ext4_chksum bridge function (!crc32c_append(!raw, data)) is verified correct for known test vectors (b'hello' produces the expected result). The GDT field offsets (bg_block_bitmap_csum_lo at 0x18, bg_block_bitmap_csum_hi at 0x38) match the kernel struct. The csum_seed derivation (from s_checksum_seed when CSUM_SEED is set) is verified correct.

POSSIBLE ROOT CAUSES:
(a) The crc32c crate 0.6.8 may have a hardware-acceleration edge case on this CPU (the test passes for small inputs but may diverge on 4096-byte inputs).
(b) There may be a subtle difference in how the kernel chains CRC32C for bitmap checksums vs how we do it (e.g., the kernel might zero some bits in the bitmap before checksumming, or exclude the last N bytes).
(c) The group descriptor checksum field offset may differ between mkfs.ext4 versions.
(d) There's a SECOND bitmap checksum function (compute_bitmap_checksum at line 1743) that uses csum_seed^group (XOR) instead of chaining — this function is wrong but verify_block_bitmap_checksum doesn't use it. However, it may have been copy-pasted from or to somewhere unexpected.

INVESTIGATION PLAN:
1. Add a test that creates a fresh ext4 image with mkfs.ext4, reads the raw bitmap bytes, and computes our CRC step-by-step, printing intermediate values.
2. Cross-validate by computing the same CRC using a second Rust CRC32C implementation (e.g., crc32c-hw or manual software CRC).
3. If the crate is correct, hexdump the raw GDT bytes and verify our parser reads the stored checksum from the right offset.
4. Check if mkfs.ext4 zeroes any bitmap bits before checksumming (e.g., unused tail bits beyond blocks_per_group).
5. Check the kernel source for ext4_block_bitmap_csum_set() to see if it modifies the bitmap before checksumming.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- ext4_bitmap_free_space_matches_kernel test passes on a fresh mkfs.ext4 image.
- The root cause is documented in a code comment.
- If the crc32c crate has a bug, switch to an alternative or add a workaround.
- The wrong compute_bitmap_checksum function (XOR-based) is either removed or clearly marked as deprecated.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:04:10.024539600Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-25T03:30:25.535625271Z","closed_at":"2026-03-25T03:30:25.535051315Z","close_reason":"Root cause: (1) group_number was chained into bitmap CRC but kernel doesn't do this, (2) checksum length was full block instead of clusters/inodes_per_group/8.  {"id":"bd-xzn0","title":"Succinct structures for free-space bitmaps (Graveyard Entry 7.1)","description":"# Succinct structures for free-space bitmaps (Graveyard Entry 7.1)

## GOAL
Replace traditional free-space bitmaps with succinct data structures (rank/select bitmaps) that support O(1) rank and select queries while using near-optimal space.

## BACKGROUND
From Alien CS Graveyard 7.1: Succinct bitmaps (Jacobson 1989, Clark 1996) store bitmaps in n + o(n) bits while supporting:
- rank(i): count set bits in positions [0, i) — O(1)
- select(k): find position of k-th set bit — O(1)

For free-space bitmaps, this enables:
- O(1) "how many free blocks in range?" (rank)
- O(1) "find k-th free block" (select) — replaces linear scan for allocation

## DESIGN
- Two-level rank structure: superblocks (every 2^16 bits) + blocks (every 2^9 bits)
- Select via rank binary search or Vigna's select structure
- Space overhead: ~6.25% over raw bitmap (vs 0% for plain bitmap, but queries are O(n))
- Integration: replace ffs-ext4 block bitmap and inode bitmap

## APPLICABILITY
- Optimization for large filesystems where bitmap scans are expensive
- Most impactful when block groups are large or filesystem is >90% full
- Could also accelerate mballoc buddy allocator queries

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Succinct bitmap with O(1) rank and select
- [ ] Space overhead < 10% over raw bitmap
- [ ] Benchmark: allocation scan time vs plain bitmap
- [ ] Drop-in replacement for block/inode bitmaps","acceptance_criteria":"Succinct bitmap with O(1) rank and select operations. Space overhead below 10% over raw bitmap. Two-level rank structure: superblocks (every 2^16 bits) plus blocks (every 2^9 bits). Drop-in replacement for block and inode bitmaps via BlockBitmap trait. Benchmark: allocation scan time vs plain bitmap at 50%, 80%, 95% fullness. Benchmark: rank/select latency vs linear scan. Unit tests: rank correctness for all-zeros, all-ones, random patterns. Unit tests: select correctness for sparse and dense bitmaps. Unit tests: round-trip serialization/deserialization.
